lessons.title: What an HDL Is: Describing Hardware, Not Software
lessons.content:
## What is an HDL?

Imagine you want a custom digital circuit built — something made of logic gates. You could draw every gate by hand, but that gets impossible fast. Instead, engineers **describe** the circuit in text. That text is written in a **Hardware Description Language (HDL)** — a language for describing digital hardware in words instead of drawings.

Here is the one idea that surprises every newcomer: **an HDL describes hardware, and hardware runs all at once.** Normal software is like a to-do list: do step 1, then step 2, then step 3, one after another. Hardware is not a to-do list. It is a physical circuit where every part is alive and reacting *at the same time* — in **parallel**. So when you write HDL, you are not writing steps to run in order. You are describing what the circuit *is*.

A good mental picture: HDL is like a **blueprint** (or a recipe a factory follows). You write what the thing should be; a tool called a **synthesizer** turns your text into real gates on a chip. Compare that to a to-do list someone follows top to bottom — that is ordinary software, not HDL.

There are two main HDLs: **Verilog** (which looks a bit like the C programming language) and **VHDL** (more wordy and strict). They do the same job. We focus on Verilog here, and VHDL exists alongside it.

Here is a tiny complete example — a module (a named block of hardware) for an AND gate:

```verilog
module and_gate(input a, input b, output y);
  assign y = a & b;   // y is always a AND b
endmodule
```

That `assign` is not a step that runs once. It is a permanent connection: whenever `a` or `b` changes, `y` follows — exactly like a real gate with wires.

**Recap:** An HDL describes parallel hardware as text. You say what the circuit IS; a synthesizer builds it. Verilog and VHDL are the two big ones.

items.prompt: What does an HDL let an engineer do?
choices: Write a program that runs on a regular CPU
Describe digital hardware (a circuit) using text
Draw gates by hand on paper only
Speed up an existing software application
items.explanation: HDL stands for Hardware Description Language. Its whole purpose is to describe a digital circuit in text so a tool can build it, instead of drawing every gate by hand.

items.prompt: What is the single biggest mindset shift when learning HDL?
choices: HDL code runs faster than normal software
HDL describes hardware that all runs at once (in parallel), not step-by-step
HDL must be written in uppercase letters
HDL can only describe a single logic gate
items.explanation: Ordinary software runs like a to-do list, one step after another. HDL describes a physical circuit where every part is alive and reacting simultaneously. This describes parallel hardware, not sequential code.

items.prompt: Which everyday analogy best fits writing HDL?
choices: A to-do list executed one item at a time
A blueprint or recipe that a factory turns into a physical thing
A phone call between two people
A spreadsheet of numbers
items.explanation: HDL is like a blueprint: you describe what the thing should be, and a tool (synthesis) builds it. A to-do list executed in order is the analogy for ordinary sequential software, which is the opposite mindset.

items.prompt: What are the two main HDLs in wide use?
choices: Python and Java
Verilog and VHDL
HTML and CSS
C and Assembly
items.explanation: Verilog (C-like) and VHDL (wordier, stricter) are the two dominant hardware description languages. Python, Java, C, and HTML are ordinary software or markup languages, not HDLs.

items.prompt: In `assign y = a & b;`, when does `y` change?
choices: Only once, the first time the code is read
Only when someone calls the module like a function
Whenever a or b changes — it is a permanent connection
Only when the computer is restarted
items.explanation: A continuous assignment describes a permanent wired connection, like a real gate. The output y always tracks a AND b; it is not a one-time step that runs and finishes.

items.prompt: What is a 'module' in Verilog?
choices: A single transistor
A named block of hardware with inputs and outputs
A type of computer file
A loop that repeats forever
items.explanation: A module is the basic building block: a named box of hardware with a list of input and output pins and a body that describes the logic inside. Designs are built from modules.

items.prompt: What tool turns your HDL text into real gates on a chip?
choices: A web browser
A synthesizer (synthesis tool)
A spreadsheet program
A text editor
items.explanation: Synthesis is the step that translates your HDL description into actual logic gates and connections. A text editor only lets you type the HDL; it does not build the hardware.

items.prompt: Why is thinking of HDL as 'a program that runs top to bottom' a mistake?
choices: Because HDL has no lines of text at all
Because the parts of a circuit are all active at the same time, not in sequence
Because HDL only works on weekends
Because HDL cannot describe outputs
items.explanation: There is no single point of execution moving down the code. The described hardware exists all at once and every piece reacts in parallel. Treating it as ordered steps leads to wrong designs.

items.prompt: Which statement about Verilog and VHDL is true?
choices: Only Verilog can describe hardware; VHDL is for software
They are two different HDLs that do the same kind of job
VHDL runs on the internet and Verilog does not
You must use both at the same time in every design
items.explanation: Both are hardware description languages used to describe digital circuits. Verilog looks C-like; VHDL is wordier and stricter. A given design is usually written in one or the other.

lessons.title: Modules, Ports, and Wires (wire vs reg)
lessons.content:
## Building blocks: modules, ports, and wires

Now that you know a **module** is a named box of hardware, let us look at how you describe its edges and its insides. Think of a module like a labeled box on a diagram. The **ports** are its pins — the wires going in and out. A **wire** is just what it sounds like: a connection that carries a signal from one place to another.

Ports come in three flavors: `input` (signal comes in), `output` (signal goes out), and `inout` (rarely, both ways). A port can carry one bit, or a whole bundle of bits called a **bus**. You write a bus width with a range like `[7:0]`, which means 8 bits numbered 7 down to 0.

Here is a module with buses:

```verilog
module adder(input  [7:0] a,
             input  [7:0] b,
             input        cin,
             output [7:0] sum,
             output       cout);
  assign {cout, sum} = a + b + cin;   // add the two 8-bit numbers
endmodule
```

The `{cout, sum}` part glues signals together (called **concatenation**) so the extra carry bit lands on top of the 8-bit sum.

Now a famous beginner confusion: **`wire` vs `reg`**. These are two ways to declare a signal in Verilog. A `wire` is a signal that is driven continuously (by an `assign` or by a module's output). A `reg` is a signal you set inside a special block (an `always` block, covered next lesson). Here is the trap: **`reg` does NOT mean it becomes a hardware register (a memory element).** The name is misleading. It is just "a signal I write to inside a block." The real difference is *how the signal is driven*, not whether it stores anything.

Modern Verilog (called **SystemVerilog**) fixes this confusion with one keyword, `logic`, that works in both situations — so you stop worrying about the choice.

One more tip: write bit widths explicitly, like `4'b1010` (4 bits, in binary) or `8'hFF` (8 bits, in hex). If widths do not match, Verilog quietly pads or chops them, which causes sneaky bugs.

**Recap:** A module is a box; ports are its pins; a wire carries a signal. `wire` vs `reg` is about *how a signal is driven*, not about storage — and SystemVerilog's `logic` sidesteps the whole thing.

items.prompt: What are a module's 'ports'?
choices: The hidden logic inside the module
Its input and output pins — the signals going in and out
The clock speed of the module
A list of bugs in the design
items.explanation: Ports are the module's interface: the input, output, and inout signals that connect it to the outside. Think of them as the pins on a labeled box.

items.prompt: What does the range `[7:0]` mean in `input [7:0] a`?
choices: The signal is 7 volts
The signal is a bus of 8 bits (numbered 7 down to 0)
The signal updates 8 times per second
There are 7 separate modules
items.explanation: `[7:0]` declares a bus 8 bits wide, with bits numbered from 7 (most significant) down to 0. A bus bundles several wires that travel together, like an 8-bit number.

items.prompt: What is a 'wire' in Verilog?
choices: A way to store a number permanently
A connection that carries a signal between points
A type of clock
An error message
items.explanation: A wire is a connection carrying a signal from one place to another, just like a physical wire. It is driven continuously, for example by an assign statement or a module output.

items.prompt: Does declaring a signal as `reg` always create a hardware memory element?
choices: Yes, reg always becomes a stored register
No — reg just means the signal is assigned inside a block; it may be plain logic
Yes, but only in VHDL
No, reg is not a real keyword
items.explanation: This is the classic trap: the name 'reg' is misleading. It only means the signal is written inside a procedural block. Whether it becomes storage depends on the block, not the keyword.

items.prompt: What really distinguishes `wire` from `reg`?
choices: wire is faster than reg
How the signal is driven (continuously vs assigned inside a block)
wire is for outputs and reg is for inputs
reg can only hold the value zero
items.explanation: The difference is the driving style: a wire is continuously driven (assign / port), while a reg is assigned inside an always/initial block. It is about driving, not about storage or speed.

items.prompt: What problem does SystemVerilog's `logic` keyword solve?
choices: It makes the chip run cooler
It works in both situations, so you stop choosing between wire and reg
It encrypts your code
It removes the need for modules
items.explanation: `logic` is a single type usable both in continuous assignments and inside blocks, removing the confusing wire-vs-reg choice. It does not affect temperature, security, or modules.

items.prompt: Why write sized literals like `8'hFF` instead of just `255`?
choices: It looks more professional only
It makes the bit width explicit, avoiding silent padding or truncation bugs
It runs faster in hardware
It is required for every number in Verilog
items.explanation: Stating the width (8 bits here) prevents Verilog from quietly extending or chopping the value to fit a mismatched size, which is a common source of hard-to-find bugs.

items.prompt: What does `{cout, sum}` do in the adder example?
choices: Comments out the line
Joins (concatenates) signals so the carry bit sits on top of the sum
Deletes the sum signal
Creates a new module
items.explanation: The curly braces concatenate signals into one wider bundle. Here the carry-out lands as the extra top bit above the 8-bit sum, capturing any overflow cleanly.

items.prompt: A signal you assign inside an `always` block is typically declared as:
choices: wire
reg (or logic in SystemVerilog)
input
module
items.explanation: Signals written inside a procedural always block must be reg (or SystemVerilog logic). A wire is only for continuous assignment and port connections, not for writing inside a block.

lessons.title: Combinational vs Sequential, and the = vs <= Rule
lessons.content:
## Two kinds of logic, and the famous beginner trap

Digital circuits come in two basic flavors, and HDL describes each a little differently.

**Combinational logic** has no memory. Its output depends only on its inputs *right now* — change an input, and the output follows immediately, like a plain AND gate. In Verilog you describe it with a continuous `assign`, or with a block written `always @(*)`. The `@(*)` means "re-check whenever any input changes." Inside that block you use the `=` operator (called **blocking**), which acts in order like ordinary code:

```verilog
always @(*) begin       // combinational: no clock
  if (sel) y = a;
  else     y = b;
end
```

**Sequential logic** has memory. It updates only on a **clock** edge — a signal that ticks, like a metronome. A storage element called a **flip-flop** grabs a new value on each tick and holds it until the next one. You describe it with `always @(posedge clk)` ("on the rising edge of the clock") and the `<=` operator (called **non-blocking**):

```verilog
always @(posedge clk) begin
  q1 <= d;            // sample everything first,
  q2 <= q1;           // then update everything together
end
```

Here is the field's number-one trap, worth memorizing as a simple rule:

- In a **clocked** block (`posedge clk`): use **non-blocking `<=`**.
- In a **combinational** block (`always @(*)`): use **blocking `=`**.

Why does it matter? Non-blocking `<=` reads *all* the right-hand sides using the values *at the clock tick*, then updates *all* the left-hand sides together. That is exactly what a row of real flip-flops does — they all grab their inputs at the same instant. The code above builds a two-stage **shift register**: `q2` gets the *old* `q1`. If you wrongly used blocking `=` there, `q2` would read the *new* `q1` and the two stages would collapse into one. Worse, it might look fine in simulation but build wrong hardware.

**Recap:** Combinational = no clock, use `=`. Sequential (clocked) = uses a flip-flop, use `<=`. Memorize the rule; it is the most common HDL beginner mistake.

items.prompt: What is combinational logic?
choices: Logic whose output depends only on its current inputs, with no memory
Logic that needs a clock to update
Logic that stores values forever
Logic that only exists in software
items.explanation: Combinational logic has no memory: its output is purely a function of the inputs at this moment, like a plain AND gate. Memory and clocks belong to sequential logic.

items.prompt: What is sequential logic?
choices: Logic with no inputs
Logic that has memory and updates on a clock edge
Logic that runs in a web browser
Logic that can only be one bit wide
items.explanation: Sequential logic remembers values and updates them on a clock edge using storage elements (flip-flops). That memory and clock dependence is what separates it from combinational logic.

items.prompt: What does a clock signal do in a digital circuit?
choices: It tells the time of day to the user
It ticks like a metronome, telling flip-flops when to update
It powers the chip
It stores the final result
items.explanation: A clock is a regularly ticking signal. On each edge, sequential elements like flip-flops sample their inputs and update. It is the heartbeat that synchronizes the hardware.

items.prompt: Inside an `always @(posedge clk)` block, which operator should you use?
choices: = (blocking)
<= (non-blocking)
assign
items.explanation: Clocked blocks describe flip-flops, so use non-blocking <=. It samples all right-hand sides at the clock edge, then updates all left-hand sides together, matching how real flip-flops behave.

items.prompt: Inside a combinational `always @(*)` block, which operator should you use?
choices: <= (non-blocking)
= (blocking)
posedge
assign
items.explanation: Combinational blocks use blocking =, which acts in order like ordinary code so intermediate values read back immediately. Non-blocking <= belongs in clocked blocks.

items.prompt: Why does `q1 <= d; q2 <= q1;` correctly build a two-stage shift register?
choices: Because <= updates q1 first, then q2 reads the new value
Because both right-hand sides use the OLD values at the clock edge, then update together
Because = would do the exact same thing
Because there is no clock involved
items.explanation: Non-blocking reads q1 and d using their pre-edge values, then updates both at once. So q2 receives the OLD q1, giving two genuine flip-flop stages, just like real hardware.

items.prompt: If you wrongly use blocking `q1 = d; q2 = q1;` in a clocked block, what usually happens?
choices: You still get a correct two-stage shift register
q2 reads the new q1, so the two stages collapse into one flip-flop
The code will not compile at all
An extra clock is created automatically
items.explanation: Blocking executes in order, so q2 reads the already-updated q1. The two stages collapse into a single register, and it can even simulate fine while building wrong hardware.

items.prompt: Which line correctly states the blocking/non-blocking discipline?
choices: Use blocking in clocked blocks, non-blocking in combinational blocks
Use non-blocking in clocked blocks, blocking in combinational blocks
Use non-blocking everywhere
Use blocking everywhere
items.explanation: The rule to memorize: non-blocking <= in clocked (posedge clk) blocks, and blocking = in combinational always @(*) blocks. This avoids the most common HDL beginner bug.

items.prompt: Why is mixing up `=` and `<=` especially dangerous?
choices: It always shows an immediate, obvious error
The design can simulate fine yet synthesize to different (wrong) hardware
It changes which pins the chip uses
It only matters in VHDL, never Verilog
items.explanation: The failure is silent. The wrong operator can pass simulation but build different hardware (or cause a race), so the bug hides until the real chip misbehaves.

items.prompt: What does `always @(*)` describe in hardware terms?
choices: A clocked flip-flop
Combinational logic that re-evaluates whenever any input changes
A simulation-only delay
A memory chip
items.explanation: The @(*) means the block re-evaluates on any input change, with no clock. That describes combinational logic, the kind whose output follows its inputs immediately.

lessons.title: Testbenches, Synthesizable vs Sim-Only, and Inferred Latches
lessons.content:
## Testing your design, and two important boundaries

Before you build hardware, you test the description in **simulation** — running the HDL on a computer to watch how it behaves, no chip required. You test it with a **testbench**: a separate piece of HDL whose only job is to feed your design inputs and check the outputs. A testbench is *simulation-only*; it is never turned into hardware. A good testbench is **self-checking**: it compares what the design produced against what you expected and reports any mismatch automatically.

```verilog
initial begin
  a = 0; b = 1; #10;        // set inputs, wait 10 time units
  if (y !== 1) $error("oops");
end
```

Things like `initial`, the `#10` delay, and `$error` are great in a testbench but **cannot become hardware**. This is the **synthesizable vs simulation-only** boundary: the part of your code meant to become a real circuit (called RTL) must avoid those simulation-only tricks. Roughly, the design is synthesizable; the testbench around it is not.

Now the second classic trap: the **accidentally inferred latch**. In a combinational `always @(*)` block, if a signal is *not* given a value on every possible path, the tool concludes it must *remember* its old value. To remember, it inserts a **latch** (a tiny unintended memory element) you did not ask for. Latches here are almost always a bug and hurt timing.

```verilog
always @(*) begin
  y = 1'b0;          // default first: no latch!
  if (sel) y = a;    // every path now sets y
end
```

The fix is simple: **give every output a default value first**, and finish every `if` with an `else` and every `case` with a `default`. Then no path leaves a signal unassigned.

Finally, modern **SystemVerilog** helps you catch these mistakes. Writing `always_comb` instead of `always @(*)` tells the tool "this is combinational" and it will *warn you* if you accidentally create a latch. Writing `always_ff @(posedge clk)` says "this is a flip-flop" and warns if you slip up. Remember too that **VHDL** exists and expresses all the same ideas in a wordier style.

**Recap:** Simulate with a self-checking testbench (sim-only); keep your real design free of sim-only tricks; avoid accidental latches by defaulting every output; and let SystemVerilog's `always_comb` / `always_ff` catch your mistakes.

items.prompt: What is a testbench?
choices: The final hardware chip
Simulation-only HDL that feeds your design inputs and checks its outputs
A physical workbench in a lab
A type of logic gate
items.explanation: A testbench is extra HDL used only in simulation to drive your design with inputs and verify the outputs. It is never synthesized into hardware itself.

items.prompt: What makes a testbench 'self-checking'?
choices: It writes itself automatically
It compares the design's output to an expected value and flags mismatches
It runs on real hardware
It needs no inputs at all
items.explanation: A self-checking testbench automatically compares actual outputs against expected ones and reports errors, instead of making you eyeball waveforms by hand.

items.prompt: What is 'simulation'?
choices: Programming a real FPGA
Running the HDL on a computer to watch its behavior, without a chip
Drawing the circuit by hand
Soldering components onto a board
items.explanation: Simulation runs your HDL as a model on a computer so you can check behavior before committing to hardware. It needs no physical chip.

items.prompt: Which construct belongs ONLY in a testbench, never in synthesizable design code?
choices: assign
always @(posedge clk)
`#10` time delays and `$error`
case statements
items.explanation: Time delays like #10, plus $display/$error and initial-for-state, are simulation-only. The design meant to become real hardware (RTL) must avoid them.

items.prompt: What does 'synthesizable' mean?
choices: The code is written in VHDL
The code can be turned into real hardware (gates and flip-flops)
The code runs faster than software
The code is only for documentation
items.explanation: Synthesizable code is the subset that a synthesis tool can convert into actual hardware. Simulation-only constructs like delays are not synthesizable.

items.prompt: What is an accidentally inferred latch?
choices: A flip-flop you deliberately added
An unwanted memory element the tool adds when a combinational signal isn't set on every path
A faster kind of clock
A testbench feature
items.explanation: If a combinational always @(*) leaves a signal unassigned on some path, the tool makes it remember its old value by inserting a latch. That is almost always an unintended bug.

items.prompt: What is the most reliable way to avoid an inferred latch?
choices: Add more clocks to the design
Give every output a default value first, and complete every if/case
Use non-blocking assignments in combinational blocks
Remove all comments
items.explanation: Assigning a default at the top of the block, plus full else/default branches, guarantees every path drives the signal, so no latch is needed to remember an old value.

items.prompt: What is the main benefit of SystemVerilog's `always_comb` and `always_ff`?
choices: They make simulation run faster
They state your intent so the tool can warn about latches or accidental misuse
They remove the need for a clock
They make wire and reg identical in every case
items.explanation: always_comb declares combinational intent and warns on inferred latches; always_ff declares flip-flop intent and warns on accidental combinational coding. They let the tool catch your mistakes.

items.prompt: Which statement about VHDL is correct?
choices: VHDL is a software-only language unrelated to hardware
VHDL is another HDL that expresses the same ideas in a wordier style
VHDL cannot describe sequential logic
VHDL has no concept of testbenches
items.explanation: VHDL is a hardware description language alongside Verilog. It is wordier and stricter but covers the same concepts: modules, ports, combinational and sequential logic, and testbenches.
